content.thinking: Let me start by exploring the codebase to understand the structure and existing code before creating a plan.

input.prompt:
Explore this FastAPI project codebase thoroughly. I need to understand:
1. The overall project structure (directories, files, config)
2. The middleware package - what's in it, how it's organized, what existing middleware exists
3. How the FastAPI app is set up and how middleware is registered
4. Existing test patterns - how tests are organized, what test utilities/fixtures exist
5. Any existing rate limiting or IP-related code
6. The pyproject.toml or requirements files to understand dependencies

Please read ALL relevant files - don't just list them, actually read the content. Focus on:
- The middleware package (all files)
- The main app setup file
- Existing test files for middleware
- Config/settings files
- pyproject.toml or requirements.txt

Be thorough and report file contents, not just summaries.

45	class FastAPI(Starlette):
46	    """
47	    `FastAPI` app class, the main entrypoint to use FastAPI.
48	
49	    Read more in the
50	    [FastAPI docs for First Steps](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/first-steps/).
51	
52	    ## Example
53	
54	    ```python
55	    from fastapi import FastAPI
56	
57	    app = FastAPI()
58	    ```
59	    """
60	
61	    def __init__(
62	        self: AppType,
63	        *,
64	        debug: Annotated[
65	            bool,
66	            Doc(
67	                """
68	                Boolean indicating if debug tracebacks should be returned on server
69	                errors.
70	
71	                Read more in the
72	                [Starlette docs for Applications](https://www.starlette.dev/applications/#instantiating-the-application).
73	                """
74	            ),
75	        ] = False,
76	        routes: Annotated[
77	            list[BaseRoute] | None,
78	            Doc(
79	                """
80	                **Note**: you probably shouldn't use this parameter, it is inherited
81	                from Starlette and supported for compatibility.
82	
83	                ---
84	
85	                A list of routes to serve incoming HTTP and WebSocket requests.
86	                """
87	            ),
88	            deprecated(
89	                """
90	                You normally wouldn't use this parameter with FastAPI, it is inherited
91	                from Starlette and supported for compatibility.
92	
93	                In FastAPI, you normally would use the *path operation methods*,
94	                like `app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc.
95	                """
96	            ),
97	        ] = None,
98	        title: Annotated[
99	            str,
100	            Doc(
101	                """
102	                The title of the API.
103	
104	                It will be added to the generated OpenAPI (e.g. visible at `/docs`).
105	
106	                Read more in the
107	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#metadata-for-api).
108	
109	                **Example**
110	
111	                ```python
112	                from fastapi import FastAPI
113	
114	                app = FastAPI(title="ChimichangApp")
115	                ```
116	                """
117	            ),
118	        ] = "FastAPI",
119	        summary: Annotated[
120	            str | None,
121	            Doc(
122	                """
123	                A short summary of the API.
124	
125	                It will be added to the generated OpenAPI (e.g. visible at `/docs`).
126	
127	                Read more in the
128	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#metadata-for-api).
129	
130	                **Example**
131	
132	                ```python
133	                from fastapi import FastAPI
134	
135	                app = FastAPI(summary="Deadpond's favorite app. Nuff said.")
136	                ```
137	                """
138	            ),
139	        ] = None,
140	        description: Annotated[
141	            str,
142	            Doc(
143	                '''
144	                A description of the API. Supports Markdown (using
145	                [CommonMark syntax](https://commonmark.org/)).
146	
147	                It will be added to the generated OpenAPI (e.g. visible at `/docs`).
148	
149	                Read more in the
150	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#metadata-for-api).
151	
152	                **Example**
153	
154	                ```python
155	                from fastapi import FastAPI
156	
157	                app = FastAPI(
158	                    description="""
159	                                ChimichangApp API helps you do awesome stuff. 🚀
160	
161	                                ## Items
162	
163	                                You can **read items**.
164	
165	                                ## Users
166	
167	                                You will be able to:
168	
169	                                * **Create users** (_not implemented_).
170	                                * **Read users** (_not implemented_).
171	
172	                                """
173	                )
174	                ```
175	                '''
176	            ),
177	        ] = "",
178	        version: Annotated[
179	            str,
180	            Doc(
181	                """
182	                The version of the API.
183	
184	                **Note** This is the version of your application, not the version of
185	                the OpenAPI specification nor the version of FastAPI being used.
186	
187	                It will be added to the generated OpenAPI (e.g. visible at `/docs`).
188	
189	                Read more in the
190	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#metadata-for-api).
191	
192	                **Example**
193	
194	                ```python
195	                from fastapi import FastAPI
196	
197	                app = FastAPI(version="0.0.1")
198	                ```
199	                """
200	            ),
201	        ] = "0.1.0",
202	        openapi_url: Annotated[
203	            str | None,
204	            Doc(
205	                """
206	                The URL where the OpenAPI schema will be served from.
207	
208	                If you set it to `None`, no OpenAPI schema will be served publicly, and
209	                the default automatic endpoints `/docs` and `/redoc` will also be
210	                disabled.
211	
212	                Read more in the
213	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#openapi-url).
214	
215	                **Example**
216	
217	                ```python
218	                from fastapi import FastAPI
219	
220	                app = FastAPI(openapi_url="/api/v1/openapi.json")
221	                ```
222	                """
223	            ),
224	        ] = "/openapi.json",
225	        openapi_tags: Annotated[
226	            list[dict[str, Any]] | None,
227	            Doc(
228	                """
229	                A list of tags used by OpenAPI, these are the same `tags` you can set
230	                in the *path operations*, like:
231	
232	                * `@app.get("/users/", tags=["users"])`
233	                * `@app.get("/items/", tags=["items"])`
234	
235	                The order of the tags can be used to specify the order shown in
236	                tools like Swagger UI, used in the automatic path `/docs`.
237	
238	                It's not required to specify all the tags used.
239	
240	                The tags that are not declared MAY be organized randomly or based
241	                on the tools' logic. Each tag name in the list MUST be unique.
242	
243	                The value of each item is a `dict` containing:
244	
245	                * `name`: The name of the tag.
246	                * `description`: A short description of the tag.
247	                    [CommonMark syntax](https://commonmark.org/) MAY be used for rich
248	                    text representation.
249	                * `externalDocs`: Additional external documentation for this tag. If
250	                    provided, it would contain a `dict` with:
251	                    * `description`: A short description of the target documentation.
252	                        [CommonMark syntax](https://commonmark.org/) MAY be used for
253	                        rich text representation.
254	                    * `url`: The URL for the target documentation. Value MUST be in
255	                        the form of a URL.
256	
257	                Read more in the
258	                [FastAPI docs for Metadata and Docs URLs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/metadata/#metadata-for-tags).
259	
260	                **Example**
261	
262	                ```python
263	                from fastapi import FastAPI
264	
265	                tags_metadata = [
266	                    {
267	                        "name": "users",
268	                        "description": "Operations with users. The **login** logic is also here.",
269	                    },
270	                    {
271	                        "name": "items",
272	                        "description": "Manage items. So _fancy_ they have their own docs.",
273	                        "externalDocs": {
274	                            "description": "Items external docs",
275	                            "url": "https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/",
276	                        },
277	                    },
278	                ]
279	
280	                app = FastAPI(openapi_tags=tags_metadata)
281	                ```
282	                """
283	            ),
284	        ] = None,
285	        servers: Annotated[
286	            list[dict[str, str | Any]] | None,
287	            Doc(
288	                """
289	                A `list` of `dict`s with connectivity information to a target server.
290	
291	                You would use it, for example, if your application is served from
292	                different domains and you want to use the same Swagger UI in the
293	                browser to interact with each of them (instead of having multiple
294	                browser tabs open). Or if you want to leave fixed the possible URLs.
295	
296	                If the servers `list` is not provided, or is an empty `list`, the
297	                `servers` property in the generated OpenAPI will be:
298	
299	                * a `dict` with a `url` value of the application's mounting point
300	                (`root_path`) if it's different from `/`.

1	from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
2	
3	from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send
4	
5	
6	# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed
7	# in their own AsyncExitStack
8	class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
9	    def __init__(
10	        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
11	    ) -> None:
12	        self.app = app
13	        self.context_name = context_name
14	
15	    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
16	        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
17	            scope[self.context_name] = stack
18	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
19

300	                (`root_path`) if it's different from `/`.
301	                * otherwise, the `servers` property will be omitted from the OpenAPI
302	                schema.
303	
304	                Each item in the `list` is a `dict` containing:
305	
306	                * `url`: A URL to the target host. This URL supports Server Variables
307	                and MAY be relative, to indicate that the host location is relative
308	                to the location where the OpenAPI document is being served. Variable
309	                substitutions will be made when a variable is named in `{`brackets`}`.
310	                * `description`: An optional string describing the host designated by
311	                the URL. [CommonMark syntax](https://commonmark.org/) MAY be used for
312	                rich text representation.
313	                * `variables`: A `dict` between a variable name and its value. The value
314	                    is used for substitution in the server's URL template.
315	
316	                Read more in the
317	                [FastAPI docs for Behind a Proxy](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/behind-a-proxy/#additional-servers).
318	
319	                **Example**
320	
321	                ```python
322	                from fastapi import FastAPI
323	
324	                app = FastAPI(
325	                    servers=[
326	                        {"url": "https://stag.example.com", "description": "Staging environment"},
327	                        {"url": "https://prod.example.com", "description": "Production environment"},
328	                    ]
329	                )
330	                ```
331	                """
332	            ),
333	        ] = None,
334	        dependencies: Annotated[
335	            Sequence[Depends] | None,
336	            Doc(
337	                """
338	                A list of global dependencies, they will be applied to each
339	                *path operation*, including in sub-routers.

1022	    def build_middleware_stack(self) -> ASGIApp:
1023	        # Duplicate/override from Starlette to add AsyncExitStackMiddleware
1024	        # inside of ExceptionMiddleware, inside of custom user middlewares
1025	        debug = self.debug
1026	        error_handler = None
1027	        exception_handlers: dict[Any, ExceptionHandler] = {}
1028	
1029	        for key, value in self.exception_handlers.items():
1030	            if key in (500, Exception):
1031	                error_handler = value
1032	            else:
1033	                exception_handlers[key] = value
1034	
1035	        middleware = (
1036	            [Middleware(ServerErrorMiddleware, handler=error_handler, debug=debug)]  # ty: ignore[invalid-argument-type]
1037	            + self.user_middleware
1038	            + [
1039	                Middleware(
1040	                    ExceptionMiddleware,  # ty: ignore[invalid-argument-type]
1041	                    handlers=exception_handlers,
1042	                    debug=debug,
1043	                ),
1044	                # Add FastAPI-specific AsyncExitStackMiddleware for closing files.
1045	                # Before this was also used for closing dependencies with yield but
1046	                # those now have their own AsyncExitStack, to properly support
1047	                # streaming responses while keeping compatibility with the previous
1048	                # versions (as of writing 0.117.1) that allowed doing
1049	                # except HTTPException inside a dependency with yield.
1050	                # This needs to happen after user middlewares because those create a
1051	                # new contextvars context copy by using a new AnyIO task group.
1052	                # This AsyncExitStack preserves the context for contextvars, not
1053	                # strictly necessary for closing files but it was one of the original
1054	                # intentions.
1055	                # If the AsyncExitStack lived outside of the custom middlewares and
1056	                # contextvars were set, for example in a dependency with 'yield'
1057	                # in that internal contextvars context, the values would not be
1058	                # available in the outer context of the AsyncExitStack.
1059	                # By placing the middleware and the AsyncExitStack here, inside all
1060	                # user middlewares, the same context is used.
1061	                # This is currently not needed, only for closing files, but used to be
1062	                # important when dependencies with yield were closed here.
1063	                Middleware(AsyncExitStackMiddleware),  # ty: ignore[invalid-argument-type]
1064	            ]
1065	        )
1066	
1067	        app = self.router
1068	        for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
1069	            app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)
1070	        return app

4688	            request: Request, call_next: Callable[[Request], Awaitable[Response]]
4689	        ) -> Response:
4690	            start_time = time.time()

4692	            process_time = time.time() - start_time
4693	            response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
4694	            return response
4695	        ```
4696	        """

25	    """Async generator with no internal await - would hang without checkpoint."""

39	
40	
41	async def _run_asgi_and_cancel(app: FastAPI, path: str, timeout: float) -> bool:
42	    """Call the ASGI app for *path* and cancel after *timeout* seconds.
43	
44	    Returns `True` if the cancellation was delivered (i.e. it did not hang).
45	    """
46	    chunks: list[bytes] = []
47	
48	    async def receive():  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
49	        # Simulate a client that never disconnects, rely on cancellation
50	        await anyio.sleep(float("inf"))
51	        return {"type": "http.disconnect"}  # pragma: no cover
52	
53	    async def send(message: dict) -> None:  # type: ignore[type-arg]
54	        if message["type"] == "http.response.body":
55	            chunks.append(message.get("body", b""))
56	
57	    scope = {
58	        "type": "http",
59	        "asgi": {"version": "3.0", "spec_version": "2.0"},
60	        "http_version": "1.1",
61	        "method": "GET",
62	        "path": path,
63	        "query_string": b"",
64	        "root_path": "",
65	        "headers": [],
66	        "server": ("test", 80),
67	    }
68	
69	    with anyio.move_on_after(timeout) as cancel_scope:
70	        await app(scope, receive, send)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
71	
72	    # If we got here within the timeout the generator was cancellable.
73	    # cancel_scope.cancelled_caught is True when move_on_after fired.
74	    return cancel_scope.cancelled_caught or len(chunks) > 0
75	
76	
77	async def test_raw_stream_cancellation() -> None:
78	    """Raw streaming endpoint should be cancellable within a reasonable time."""
79	    cancelled = await _run_asgi_and_cancel(app, "/stream-raw", timeout=3.0)
80	    # The key assertion: we reached this line at all (didn't hang).
81	    # cancelled will be True because the infinite generator was interrupted.
82	    assert cancelled
83	
84	
85	async def test_jsonl_stream_cancellation() -> None:
86	    """JSONL streaming endpoint should be cancellable within a reasonable time."""
87	    cancelled = await _run_asgi_and_cancel(app, "/stream-jsonl", timeout=3.0)
88	    assert cancelled

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54	
55	
56	@router.post("/middleware")
57	def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
58	    return {"message": "OK"}
59	
60	
61	app.include_router(router)
62	app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
63	
64	
65	client = TestClient(app)

35	class UJSONResponse(JSONResponse):
36	    """JSON response using the ujson library to serialize data to JSON.
37	
38	    **Deprecated**: `UJSONResponse` is deprecated. FastAPI now serializes data
39	    directly to JSON bytes via Pydantic when a return type or response model is
40	    set, which is faster and doesn't need a custom response class.
41	
42	    Read more in the
43	    [FastAPI docs for Custom Response](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/custom-response/#orjson-or-response-model)
44	    and the
45	    [FastAPI docs for Response Model](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/response-model/).
46	
47	    **Note**: `ujson` is not included with FastAPI and must be installed
48	    separately, e.g. `pip install ujson`.
49	    """
50	
51	    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
52	        assert ujson is not None, "ujson must be installed to use UJSONResponse"
53	        return ujson.dumps(content, ensure_ascii=False).encode("utf-8")

5	    body = openapi["paths"][path]["post"]["requestBody"]

1	import time
2	
3	from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
4	
5	app = FastAPI()
6	
7	
8	@app.middleware("http")
9	async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
10	    start_time = time.perf_counter()
11	    response = await call_next(request)
12	    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
13	    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
14	    return response

## FastAPI Project Codebase Exploration Report

I've thoroughly explored the FastAPI project codebase and gathered comprehensive information on the project structure, middleware setup, testing patterns, and configuration. Here's the detailed report:

---

### 1. Overall Project Structure

**Project:** FastAPI Framework v0.135.3
**Repository Root:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471842863-tueuc6/`

**Key Directory Structure:**
```
fastapi/
├── __init__.py                 # Main package entry point
├── applications.py             # FastAPI main class
├── middleware/                 # Middleware package (all files read)
│   ├── __init__.py            # Re-exports: Middleware from Starlette
│   ├── asyncexitstack.py      # Custom AsyncExitStackMiddleware for closing files
│   ├── cors.py                # Re-exports: CORSMiddleware from Starlette
│   ├── gzip.py                # Re-exports: GZipMiddleware from Starlette
│   ├── httpsredirect.py       # Re-exports: HTTPSRedirectMiddleware from Starlette
│   ├── trustedhost.py         # Re-exports: TrustedHostMiddleware from Starlette
│   └── wsgi.py                # Re-exports: WSGIMiddleware from Starlette
├── dependencies/              # Dependency injection system
├── security/                   # Security utilities (OAuth2, API Key, HTTP auth)
├── routing.py                 # APIRouter implementation
├── requests.py                # Re-exports: Request from Starlette
├── responses.py               # Response classes
└── [other modules...]         # encoders, exceptions, logger, utils, etc.

tests/
├── test_application.py        # Main app tests with TestClient
├── test_custom_middleware_exception.py  # Example middleware test
├── test_request_params/       # Comprehensive parameter validation tests
├── main.py                    # Test fixture app (not a test file, shared app for tests)
└── [200+ test files]

docs_src/
├── middleware/                # Basic middleware examples
│   └── tutorial001_py310.py  # @app.middleware("http") decorator example
├── advanced_middleware/       # Advanced middleware examples
│   ├── tutorial001_py310.py  # HTTPSRedirectMiddleware example
│   ├── tutorial002_py310.py  # TrustedHostMiddleware example
│   └── tutorial003_py310.py  # GZipMiddleware example

pyproject.toml                 # Project config (read below)
```

---

### 2. The Middleware Package - Detailed Analysis

**File: `/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`**
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```
This simply re-exports Starlette's `Middleware` class. All middleware in FastAPI are wrappers/re-exports of Starlette middleware.

**Existing Middleware Files:**

1. **`asyncexitstack.py`** - CUSTOM TO FASTAPI
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```
**Purpose:** Manages file closure after requests and dependency cleanup. Used internally by FastAPI's middleware stack.

2. **`cors.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
3. **`gzip.py`** - Re-export from Starlette  
4. **`httpsredirect.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
5. **`trustedhost.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
6. **`wsgi.py`** - Re-export from Starlette

All other middleware are thin wrappers that re-export Starlette implementations.

---

### 3. How FastAPI App is Set Up & Middleware Registration

**File: `/fastapi/applications.py` (Lines 45-1070)**

**FastAPI Class Inherits from Starlette:**
```python
class FastAPI(Starlette):
```

**`__init__` Constructor Signature (Key parts):**
- Lines 61-875: Full parameter list with 50+ parameters
- **Key middleware-related parameters:**
  - `middleware` (lines 473-486): List of `Middleware` objects to add at app creation
  - Accepts `Sequence[Middleware] | None = None`

**Middleware Registration Methods:**

1. **`build_middleware_stack()` (lines 1022-1070)** - Core middleware building logic
   
```python
def build_middleware_stack(self) -> ASGIApp:
    # Duplicate/override from Starlette to add AsyncExitStackMiddleware
    # inside of ExceptionMiddleware, inside of custom user middlewares
    debug = self.debug
    error_handler = None
    exception_handlers: dict[Any, ExceptionHandler] = {}

    for key, value in self.exception_handlers.items():
        if key in (500, Exception):
            error_handler = value
        else:
            exception_handlers[key] = value

    middleware = (
        [Middleware(ServerErrorMiddleware, handler=error_handler, debug=debug)]
        + self.user_middleware  # User-added middleware goes here
        + [
            Middleware(
                ExceptionMiddleware,
                handlers=exception_handlers,
                debug=debug,
            ),
            # FastAPI-specific AsyncExitStackMiddleware
            Middleware(AsyncExitStackMiddleware),
        ]
    )

    app = self.router
    for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
        app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)
    return app
```

**Key Points:**
- Middleware stack order (from outermost to innermost):
  1. ServerErrorMiddleware (catches 500 errors)
  2. User-added middleware (via `add_middleware()` or constructor)
  3. ExceptionMiddleware (handles HTTPException)
  4. AsyncExitStackMiddleware (closes files/resources)
  5. Router (actual route handlers)

2. **`middleware()` Decorator (lines 4658-4702)** - Simple decorator wrapper

```python
def middleware(self, middleware_type: str) -> Callable:
    """
    Add a middleware to the application.
    Example: @app.middleware("http")
    """
    def decorator(func: DecoratedCallable) -> DecoratedCallable:
        self.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)
        return func
    return decorator
```

**Note:** This inherits `add_middleware()` from Starlette base class (not shown in FastAPI code, but used throughout).

---

### 4. Existing Test Patterns

**File: `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`** (Complete example of middleware testing)

```python
from pathlib import Path
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

# 1. Define custom middleware class (ASGI-compatible)
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(422, detail={...})
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)

# 2. Create app and router
app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()

# 3. Add route
@router.post("/middleware")
def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    return {"message": "OK"}

app.include_router(router)

# 4. Register middleware with app.add_middleware()
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)

# 5. Create test client
client = TestClient(app)

# 6. Write tests using pytest
def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    # Create test file
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (2**16 + 1))
    
    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }

def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")
    
    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}
```

**Test Patterns Identified:**

1. **App Creation:** Simple `app = FastAPI()` in test files
2. **TestClient Usage:** `from fastapi.testclient import TestClient`
3. **Testing:** Using pytest with standard assertions
4. **Fixtures:** Some tests use `tmp_path` (pytest built-in fixture)
5. **Parametrization:** `@pytest.mark.parametrize()` for testing multiple paths
6. **Testing Dependencies:** Files in `tests/test_request_params/test_form/utils.py` show utility functions

**File: `/tests/test_request_params/test_form/utils.py`**
```python
def get_body_model_name(openapi: dict[str, Any], path: str) -> str:
    body = openapi["paths"][path]["post"]["requestBody"]
    body_schema = body["content"]["application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]["schema"]
    return body_schema.get("$ref", "").split("/")[-1]
```

**Test Organization:** 
- Tests are organized by category: `test_request_params/`, `test_form/`, `test_cookie/`, etc.
- Each test file is standalone with its own FastAPI app instance
- Tests use `TestClient` from `fastapi.testclient` (which wraps Starlette's TestClient)

---

### 5. Rate Limiting & IP-Related Code

**Finding:** There is **NO existing rate limiting or IP-specific middleware** in the FastAPI framework.

Search results for:
- `grep -r "rate.limit\|rate_limit\|ratelimit\|RateLimit"` - No matches
- `grep -r "client.host\|client_host\|remote.addr\|x-forwarded\|X-Forwarded"` - No matches

This means rate limiting would need to be implemented from scratch as a new middleware.

**For IP address access in custom middleware:**
- Use `scope["client"]` which is a tuple `(host, port)`
- Or access `request.client` if using the Request object
- Example from Starlette source: `request.client.host`

---

### 6. Dependencies & Configuration

**File: `/pyproject.toml` (Key sections)**

```toml
[project]
name = "fastapi"
version = "0.135.3"  # Dynamic from fastapi/__init__.py
requires-python = ">=3.10"

dependencies = [
    "starlette>=0.46.0",         # Core ASGI framework
    "pydantic>=2.9.0",           # Data validation
    "typing-extensions>=4.8.0",  # Type annotations
    "typing-inspection>=0.4.2",  # Type introspection
    "annotated-doc>=0.0.2",      # Documentation for Annotated
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
standard = [
    "fastapi-cli[standard]>=0.0.8",
    "httpx>=0.23.0,<1.0.0",      # HTTP client for TestClient
    "jinja2>=3.1.5",             # Templates
    "python-multipart>=0.0.18",  # Form data
    "email-validator>=2.0.0",
    "uvicorn[standard]>=0.12.0", # ASGI server
    "pydantic-settings>=2.0.0",
    "pydantic-extra-types>=2.0.0",
]

[dependency-groups]
tests = [
    "pytest>=9.0.0",             # Test runner
    "pytest-codspeed>=4.2.0",    # Performance testing
    "coverage[toml]>=7.13,<8.0", # Code coverage
    "dirty-equals>=0.9.0",       # Flexible assertions
    "httpx>=0.23.0,<1.0.0",      # HTTP client
    "anyio[trio]>=3.2.1,<5.0.0", # Async compatibility
    # ... many more test dependencies
]

[tool.pytest]
minversion = "9.0"
addopts = [
    "--strict-config",
    "--strict-markers",
    "--ignore=docs_src",
]
strict_xfail = true
filterwarnings = ["error"]
timeout = "20"  # 20 second timeout per test
```

**Key Test Dependencies:**
- pytest (test framework)
- httpx (HTTP testing client)
- dirty-equals (flexible assertions)
- pytest-codspeed (performance testing)
- coverage (code coverage measurement)

---

### 7. Middleware Examples from Documentation

**Basic Usage Pattern (`/docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py`):**
```python
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

**Advanced Usage Pattern (`/docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial001_py310.py`):**
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.httpsredirect import HTTPSRedirectMiddleware

app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(HTTPSRedirectMiddleware)

@app.get("/")
async def main():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```

---

### 8. Key Files Summary

| File Path | Purpose | Size |
|-----------|---------|------|
| `/fastapi/applications.py` | FastAPI main class, middleware registration | ~4700+ lines |
| `/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` | Middleware package exports | 2 lines |
| `/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py` | Custom middleware for resource cleanup | 18 lines |
| `/fastapi/middleware/cors.py` | CORS middleware wrapper | 1 line |
| `/fastapi/middleware/gzip.py` | Gzip middleware wrapper | 1 line |
| `/fastapi/middleware/httpsredirect.py` | HTTPS redirect wrapper | 3 lines |
| `/fastapi/middleware/trustedhost.py` | Trusted host middleware wrapper | 3 lines |
| `/fastapi/middleware/wsgi.py` | WSGI adapter middleware wrapper | 3 lines |
| `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py` | Example middleware testing | 95 lines |
| `/tests/test_application.py` | Main app tests | 100+ lines |
| `/tests/main.py` | Shared test fixture app | 200+ lines |
| `/pyproject.toml` | Project configuration | 342 lines |

---

### 9. Important Implementation Details

**Middleware Stack Architecture:**
- FastAPI overrides Starlette's `build_middleware_stack()` method to insert `AsyncExitStackMiddleware` in a specific position
- Middleware executes in **reverse order** of registration (as with most ASGI middleware)
- The AsyncExitStackMiddleware is positioned **inside** the ExceptionMiddleware but **outside** the router

**Request Object Structure:**
- `request.client` returns `(host, port)` tuple or None
- `request.client.host` gives the client IP
- Headers available via `request.headers`
- Scope available via `request.scope` (raw ASGI scope dict)

**ASGI Middleware Pattern:**
```python
class MyMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp):
        self.app = app
    
    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        # Process HTTP request/response
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

This comprehensive analysis provides everything needed to understand how FastAPI handles middleware, how to write new middleware, and how to properly test it.

### 2. The Middleware Package - Detailed Analysis

**File: `/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`**
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```
This simply re-exports Starlette's `Middleware` class. All middleware in FastAPI are wrappers/re-exports of Starlette middleware.

**Existing Middleware Files:**

1. **`asyncexitstack.py`** - CUSTOM TO FASTAPI
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```
**Purpose:** Manages file closure after requests and dependency cleanup. Used internally by FastAPI's middleware stack.

2. **`cors.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
3. **`gzip.py`** - Re-export from Starlette  
4. **`httpsredirect.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
5. **`trustedhost.py`** - Re-export from Starlette
6. **`wsgi.py`** - Re-export from Starlette

All other middleware are thin wrappers that re-export Starlette implementations.

### 3. How FastAPI App is Set Up & Middleware Registration

**File: `/fastapi/applications.py` (Lines 45-1070)**

**FastAPI Class Inherits from Starlette:**
```python
class FastAPI(Starlette):
```

**`__init__` Constructor Signature (Key parts):**
- Lines 61-875: Full parameter list with 50+ parameters
- **Key middleware-related parameters:**
  - `middleware` (lines 473-486): List of `Middleware` objects to add at app creation
  - Accepts `Sequence[Middleware] | None = None`

**Middleware Registration Methods:**

1. **`build_middleware_stack()` (lines 1022-1070)** - Core middleware building logic
   
```python
def build_middleware_stack(self) -> ASGIApp:
    # Duplicate/override from Starlette to add AsyncExitStackMiddleware
    # inside of ExceptionMiddleware, inside of custom user middlewares
    debug = self.debug
    error_handler = None
    exception_handlers: dict[Any, ExceptionHandler] = {}

    for key, value in self.exception_handlers.items():
        if key in (500, Exception):
            error_handler = value
        else:
            exception_handlers[key] = value

    middleware = (
        [Middleware(ServerErrorMiddleware, handler=error_handler, debug=debug)]
        + self.user_middleware  # User-added middleware goes here
        + [
            Middleware(
                ExceptionMiddleware,
                handlers=exception_handlers,
                debug=debug,
            ),
            # FastAPI-specific AsyncExitStackMiddleware
            Middleware(AsyncExitStackMiddleware),
        ]
    )

    app = self.router
    for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
        app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)
    return app
```

**Key Points:**
- Middleware stack order (from outermost to innermost):
  1. ServerErrorMiddleware (catches 500 errors)
  2. User-added middleware (via `add_middleware()` or constructor)
  3. ExceptionMiddleware (handles HTTPException)
  4. AsyncExitStackMiddleware (closes files/resources)
  5. Router (actual route handlers)

2. **`middleware()` Decorator (lines 4658-4702)** - Simple decorator wrapper

```python
def middleware(self, middleware_type: str) -> Callable:
    """
    Add a middleware to the application.
    Example: @app.middleware("http")
    """
    def decorator(func: DecoratedCallable) -> DecoratedCallable:
        self.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)
        return func
    return decorator
```

**Note:** This inherits `add_middleware()` from Starlette base class (not shown in FastAPI code, but used throughout).

### 4. Existing Test Patterns

**File: `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`** (Complete example of middleware testing)

```python
from pathlib import Path
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

# 1. Define custom middleware class (ASGI-compatible)
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(422, detail={...})
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)

# 2. Create app and router
app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()

# 3. Add route
@router.post("/middleware")
def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    return {"message": "OK"}

app.include_router(router)

# 4. Register middleware with app.add_middleware()
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)

# 5. Create test client
client = TestClient(app)

# 6. Write tests using pytest
def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    # Create test file
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (2**16 + 1))
    
    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }

def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")
    
    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}
```

**Test Patterns Identified:**

1. **App Creation:** Simple `app = FastAPI()` in test files
2. **TestClient Usage:** `from fastapi.testclient import TestClient`
3. **Testing:** Using pytest with standard assertions
4. **Fixtures:** Some tests use `tmp_path` (pytest built-in fixture)
5. **Parametrization:** `@pytest.mark.parametrize()` for testing multiple paths
6. **Testing Dependencies:** Files in `tests/test_request_params/test_form/utils.py` show utility functions

**File: `/tests/test_request_params/test_form/utils.py`**
```python
def get_body_model_name(openapi: dict[str, Any], path: str) -> str:
    body = openapi["paths"][path]["post"]["requestBody"]
    body_schema = body["content"]["application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]["schema"]
    return body_schema.get("$ref", "").split("/")[-1]
```

**Test Organization:** 
- Tests are organized by category: `test_request_params/`, `test_form/`, `test_cookie/`, etc.
- Each test file is standalone with its own FastAPI app instance
- Tests use `TestClient` from `fastapi.testclient` (which wraps Starlette's TestClient)

### 5. Rate Limiting & IP-Related Code

**Finding:** There is **NO existing rate limiting or IP-specific middleware** in the FastAPI framework.

Search results for:
- `grep -r "rate.limit\|rate_limit\|ratelimit\|RateLimit"` - No matches
- `grep -r "client.host\|client_host\|remote.addr\|x-forwarded\|X-Forwarded"` - No matches

This means rate limiting would need to be implemented from scratch as a new middleware.

**For IP address access in custom middleware:**
- Use `scope["client"]` which is a tuple `(host, port)`
- Or access `request.client` if using the Request object
- Example from Starlette source: `request.client.host`

### 7. Middleware Examples from Documentation

**Basic Usage Pattern (`/docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py`):**
```python
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

**Advanced Usage Pattern (`/docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial001_py310.py`):**

from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(HTTPSRedirectMiddleware)

@app.get("/")
async def main():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```

### 9. Important Implementation Details

**Middleware Stack Architecture:**
- FastAPI overrides Starlette's `build_middleware_stack()` method to insert `AsyncExitStackMiddleware` in a specific position
- Middleware executes in **reverse order** of registration (as with most ASGI middleware)
- The AsyncExitStackMiddleware is positioned **inside** the ExceptionMiddleware but **outside** the router

**Request Object Structure:**
- `request.client` returns `(host, port)` tuple or None
- `request.client.host` gives the client IP
- Headers available via `request.headers`
- Scope available via `request.scope` (raw ASGI scope dict)

**ASGI Middleware Pattern:**
```python
class MyMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp):
        self.app = app
    
    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        # Process HTTP request/response
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

This comprehensive analysis provides everything needed to understand how FastAPI handles middleware, how to write new middleware, and how to properly test it.

from unittest.mock import Mock, patch

def test_get_db(module: ModuleType):
    app = FastAPI()

    @app.get("/")
    def read_root(c: Annotated[Any, Depends(module.dependency_c)]):
        return {"c": str(c)}

    client = TestClient(app)

    a_mock = Mock()
    b_mock = Mock()
    c_mock = Mock()

    with (
        patch(
            f"{module.__name__}.generate_dep_a",
            return_value=a_mock,
            create=True,
        ),
        patch(
            f"{module.__name__}.generate_dep_b",
            return_value=b_mock,
            create=True,
        ),
        patch(
            f"{module.__name__}.generate_dep_c",
            return_value=c_mock,
            create=True,
        ),
    ):
        response = client.get("/")

    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == {"c": str(c_mock)}
```

def test_async_generator_with_model(client: TestClient):
    response = client.get("/items/stream")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.headers["content-type"] == "text/event-stream; charset=utf-8"
    lines = response.text.strip().split("\n")
    data_lines = [line for line in lines if line.startswith("data: ")]
    assert len(data_lines) == 3
```

**Pattern 4 - Security/OAuth Tests** (from `/tests/test_security_oauth2.py`):
```python

    flows={
        "password": {

    return user

def test_security_oauth2():

    assert response.status_code == 200
```

### 10. KEY TESTING LIBRARIES & TOOLS

|---------|-------|
| `pytest` | Test framework |
| `fastapi.testclient.TestClient` | ASGI test client |

| `inline-snapshot` | Snapshot testing |
| `unittest.mock` | Mocking/patching |
| `pytest-xdist` | Parallel test execution |

| `pytest-timeout` | Test timeout enforcement |

---

### 11. RESPONSE MODEL USAGE

**Request Parameter Types:**
- `Query()` - Query parameters
- `Path()` - Path parameters
- `Body()` - Request body
- `Form()` - Form data
- `File()` - File uploads
- `Header()` - Headers
- `Cookie()` - Cookies
- `Depends()` - Dependency injection
- `Security()` - Security dependencies

**All imported from:** `fastapi` module

---

### 12. COMMON TEST ASSERTIONS

```python

assert response.status_code == 422  # Validation error

# JSON responses

# Headers

# Using dirty-equals for fuzzy matching

assert response.json() == {
    "detail": [

            "type": "missing",

        }
    ]
}

# OpenAPI schema

# Text content

```

---

### 13. FIXTURE DEPENDENCY CHAINS

Example showing dependency injection in fixtures:

```python

def test_example(

):
    # Test code
    pass
```

---

### Summary

The FastAPI test suite demonstrates:

1. **Comprehensive Testing:** 484 test files covering all features
2. **Test Client Setup:** Simple, clean use of Starlette's TestClient
3. **Async Support:** Both `pytest.mark.anyio` and `asyncio.run()` patterns
4. **Rich Fixtures:** App, client, and state fixtures with proper dependency injection
5. **Parametrization:** Extensive use of `pytest.mark.parametrize` and indirect fixtures

[tool.pytest]

]

### 3. TEST CLIENT SETUP

**Test Client Source:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471842863-tueuc6/fastapi/testclient.py`

    app = FastAPI()

    testing_file_store: list[UploadFile] = []

    @app.post("/uploadfile/")
    def create_upload_file(file: UploadFile):
        testing_file_store.append(file)

    client = TestClient(app)

```

            return_value=b_mock,
            create=True,

        patch(

            return_value=c_mock,

    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == {"c": str(c_mock)}

file.content:
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack

from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send


# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed
# in their own AsyncExitStack
class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)

1	from pathlib import Path
2	
3	from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
4	from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
5	from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
6	
7	app = FastAPI()
8	
9	router = APIRouter()
10	
11	
12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54	
55	
56	@router.post("/middleware")
57	def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
58	    return {"message": "OK"}
59	
60	
61	app.include_router(router)
62	app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
63	
64	
65	client = TestClient(app)
66	
67	
68	def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
69	    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
70	    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
71	    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))
72	
73	    with client:
74	        with open(path, "rb") as file:
75	            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
76	        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
77	        assert response.json() == {
78	            "detail": {

from pathlib import Path

from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()

router = APIRouter()


class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
    Args:
      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
    """

    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0

        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message  # pragma: no cover

            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(
                    422,
                    detail={
                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                        "code": 999,
                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
                    },
                )
            return message

        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return

        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)


@router.post("/middleware")
def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
    return {"message": "OK"}


app.include_router(router)
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)


client = TestClient(app)


def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }


def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}

Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. Let me design the implementation plan for the token bucket rate limiting middleware.

## Token Bucket Algorithm Overview

The token bucket algorithm works like this:
- Each client (IP) has a "bucket" that holds tokens
- The bucket has a maximum capacity (burst size)
- Tokens are added at a fixed rate (requests per minute)
- Each request consumes one token
- If no tokens available → reject with 429

Implementation details:
- `capacity` = burst_size (max tokens the bucket can hold)


- `tokens` = current available tokens (starts at capacity)
- `refill_rate` = requests_per_minute / 60 (tokens per second)
- `last_refill` = timestamp of last refill

For each request, I calculate the elapsed time since the last refill, add back tokens proportional to that elapsed time (capped at capacity), and then either allow the request if tokens are available or reject it with a 429 status. I'll also include rate limit headers like `X-RateLimit-Limit` and `X-RateLimit-Remaining` to inform clients about their quota status and include a `Retry-After` header when requests are rejected.

Now I'm planning the implementation structure. I'll create a new middleware class in `fastapi/middleware/ratelimit.py` that uses a token bucket algorithm, update the middleware exports, and add comprehensive tests. The core `TokenBucket` class will handle the thread-safe token management...

Implementing token bucket logic... Writing the middleware logic... Writing response headers...

Actually, I'm noticing a potential threading issue—the test client might run synchronously while `asyncio.Lock()` requires an async context tied to an event loop. In ASGI everything shares the same event loop so that should work, but I need to think through whether the bucket implementation handles this correctly. Implementing the bucket access without a lock...

Since we're in a single-threaded async context, there's no race condition between checking if an IP exists in the dictionary and creating a new bucket—there's no `await` point in between, so the operation is atomic. For the token consumption itself, I need to make sure the read-compute-write cycle happens atomically, which means either keeping it synchronous or using a lock if I add any `await` calls.

Refactoring the consume method... I'm realizing this approach works fine in a single-threaded async context since there's no `await` within `consume()`, so only one coroutine can execute it at a time. Now I need to think through the response headers—specifically, `X-RateLimit-Remaining` should reflect the token count after consumption, and while other requests could theoretically change this during processing, it's acceptable for a rate limiter. I'm also considering what `X-RateLimit-Limit` should represent...

Calculating header values... I'm concerned about thread safety when reading `bucket.tokens` in the response handler, since concurrent requests might modify it between when we capture the value and when we use it. However, in a single-threaded async event loop, this should be safe as long as the read operation itself is atomic, which it is in Python. The real consideration is that the token count shown in the response headers might not perfectly reflect the actual state due to concurrent requests, but that's acceptable for a rate limiter's purposes.

Now I need to import `MutableHeaders` from Starlette to handle modifying the response headers, and then set up a test file with fixtures and test cases for the rate limit middleware.

Writing test cases... Writing test cases... Writing test cases...

I'm realizing the TestClient always connects from localhost, so I need a different approach to test per-IP rate limiting—either mocking the client IP or using a different testing strategy that properly simulates requests from different addresses. Looking at what's actually testable with Starlette's TestClient, I should focus on verifying that rate limiting enforces the burst limit, headers are included in responses, Retry-After appears on 429s, the remaining count decrements properly, and different configurations apply correctly. For per-IP isolation, since all TestClient requests come from the same address, I'd need to either mock the scope directly or test the internal bucket dictionary to verify that separate IPs would be tracked independently. I'm settling on testing the core functionality directly: verifying that rate limiting triggers a 429 response when the burst limit is exhausted, that response headers are always included, and that different client IPs maintain separate rate limit buckets by simulating requests with different IP addresses in the scope. Writing a test helper function... Writing the rate limit middleware... Writing the middleware class... Writing the rate limit response... I'm noticing a potential issue with the `time_to_full` calculation when the refill rate is zero—if `requests_per_minute` is zero, we'd hit a division by zero error, so I should add validation to handle this edge case gracefully.

Handling edge cases in token bucket logic...

Rather than trying to handle division by zero with infinity values, I'll add validation in the constructor to ensure both `requests_per_minute` and `burst_size` are positive integers, which prevents these edge cases from occurring in the first place.

Now I have a complete understanding of the codebase. Let me now design the implementation plan.

## Summary of Key Findings

1. **Project**: FastAPI v0.135.3, Python 3.10+
2. **Middleware package**: `fastapi/middleware/` - contains `__init__.py` (exports `Middleware` from Starlette), `asyncexitstack.py` (custom middleware), and re-export wrappers for Starlette middleware
3. **Custom middleware pattern**: ASGI-style class middleware (see `asyncexitstack.py` as template)


4. **Tests**: Each test file has its own app instance, uses `TestClient` from `fastapi.testclient`, no global conftest.py
5. **No existing rate limiting code**

## Implementation Plan

I need to build three main components: a token bucket rate limiter middleware in `fastapi/middleware/ratelimit.py`, update the middleware exports, and create comprehensive tests. The token bucket algorithm will maintain a bucket per IP address where tokens are replenished at a fixed rate, allowing bursts up to the bucket capacity while enforcing a sustained rate limit.

For the middleware implementation, I'll create an ASGI-compatible class that tracks token buckets per client IP, uses async locks for thread safety, extracts the client IP from the request scope or `X-Forwarded-For` header, and adds rate limit headers to responses.

When a request exceeds the limit, I'll return a 429 status with a JSON error message and `Retry-After` header. I also need to handle bucket cleanup to prevent memory leaks from stale IP entries.

Building the token bucket implementation... Implementing the token consumption logic... Writing the rate limiter middleware... Writing the response middleware...

Now I'm thinking through the rate limit header details—`X-RateLimit-Limit` should represent the rate limit itself rather than burst capacity, `X-RateLimit-Remaining` needs to be an integer without fractional tokens, and `X-RateLimit-Reset` should indicate when the bucket refills or when the next token becomes available depending on whether the request was allowed. I also need to add `Retry-After` headers specifically for 429 responses. There's a potential issue with asyncio lock creation timing—if the lock is instantiated during an ASIO request rather than at startup, it could cause problems with the event loop context.

Actually, in Python 3.10+, `asyncio.Lock()` works fine without a running event loop, so creating locks in the middleware's `__init__` is safe. Now I'm looking at how to inject these headers into the response using Starlette's `MutableHeaders` to modify the response message before it's sent. For the package exports, I need to follow the existing pattern of re-exporting middleware with `# noqa` annotations, though this new rate limiter is defined locally rather than imported from Starlette.

Setting up the test structure...

I'm creating test functions to verify the rate limit middleware works correctly—checking that the response headers are present, that the limit value matches the configured requests per minute, and that the remaining count decrements properly with each request.

Now I'm testing what happens when the burst size is exceeded, making multiple requests to exhaust the allowance and then verifying that the next request gets a 429 status code.

Testing the response body structure for rate-limited requests... Writing test assertions...

I need to figure out how to simulate different client IPs in the tests. The TestClient in Starlette sets the client tuple through the ASGI transport, so I can pass an `X-Forwarded-For` header to simulate different IP addresses. Then I can verify that each IP gets its own separate rate limit bucket rather than sharing one global limit.

Defining the token bucket class...

For the rate limit reset header, I need to decide what "reset" means in a token bucket context—either when the bucket refills completely or when enough tokens accumulate for the next request. The standard approach would be to calculate the Unix timestamp based on when the next token becomes available. For a token bucket, the natural reset point is when it refills to full capacity, which I can calculate by determining how many tokens are needed and dividing by the refill rate to get the timestamp. For 429 responses, `retry_after` tells the client when the next token arrives, while `reset` indicates when the bucket reaches full capacity. Now I need to address a concurrency issue: if multiple requests from the same IP arrive simultaneously, they could both bypass the bucket lookup and create duplicates, so I'll need to use a lock to ensure thread-safe bucket creation.

Writing the rate limit test... Writing the token bucket class... Setting up the middleware class with initialization parameters for the rate limiter, converting the requests-per-minute value to tokens per second, and preparing data structures to track token buckets for each client IP address along with a lock for thread-safe access.

Now I'm implementing the helper methods to extract the client IP from the request scope, checking for X-Forwarded-For headers first before falling back to the direct client connection, and then creating a lazy-loaded token bucket system that retrieves or initializes a bucket for each unique IP address.

Now I'm handling the main middleware logic by checking if this is an HTTP request, extracting the client IP, consuming tokens from the bucket, and calculating rate limit headers including the reset time based on when the bucket will refill.

If the request exceeds the limit, I'm returning a 429 response with the appropriate retry information and headers. Otherwise, I'm wrapping the send function to inject the rate limit headers into the response before it gets sent to the client.

Now I'm reconsidering how to calculate the reset time more carefully. When the bucket is nearly full, the time until it refills should account for how many tokens are needed to reach capacity and divide by the rate. If the bucket is already at capacity, the reset time should be essentially now, which makes sense.

For the 429 response when rate limiting kicks in, I should set the reset header to when the next token becomes available—that's `now + retry_after`—rather than when the bucket is completely full. Let me adjust the response to include the proper reset time calculation and headers.

For successful requests, I need to calculate when the bucket will be refilled to capacity. If there are still tokens remaining, the reset time is the current time plus however long it takes to refill from the current level to the burst size. If the bucket is already full, the reset time is just now.

Now I'm thinking about how to test per-IP rate limiting with the TestClient. By default, all TestClient requests appear to come from the same client address, so I'll need to either inject different IPs via X-Forwarded-For headers or mock the IP detection method to simulate requests from different sources.

Setting up the test file structure... Writing test cases... Writing test cases... I'm realizing HTTP headers are case-insensitive, and httpx normalizes them to lowercase, so accessing `response.headers["x-ratelimit-limit"]` works fine for the `X-RateLimit-Limit` header. Now I need to export the RateLimitMiddleware from the fastapi middleware module and think through edge cases like what happens if `requests_per_minute` is set to zero.

Looking at potential issues: zero requests per minute could cause division by zero, burst_size of zero means every request gets rejected, unknown IPs all share a bucket, and the buckets dictionary could grow indefinitely without cleanup—though for now I'll just note this limitation rather than implement complex LRU cache logic. There's also the risk of clients manipulating the X-Forwarded-For header to bypass rate limiting.

Now I'm planning out the actual implementation: I need to create the middleware file and tests, update the middleware exports, and ensure the token bucket algorithm uses proper locking for thread safety while returning the standard rate limit headers on all responses and a JSON 429 response with retry information when limits are exceeded.

I need you to design an implementation plan for adding a token bucket rate limiting middleware to a FastAPI project. Here is everything you need to know.

## Project Context

- **Project**: FastAPI v0.135.3, Python 3.10+
- **Location**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775471842863-tueuc6/`
- **Build/Test**: PDM, pytest ≥ 9.0, `filterwarnings = ["error"]` (all warnings are errors), 20s timeout per test

## Existing Middleware Package Structure

`fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` currently only contains:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

`fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py` (the only custom middleware, use as template):
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

All other middleware files (cors.py, gzip.py, trustedhost.py, etc.) are single-line re-exports from Starlette.

## Test Patterns

- Each test file creates its own `FastAPI()` app instance
- `TestClient` from `fastapi.testclient` (re-export of Starlette's TestClient, backed by httpx)
- No global conftest.py - fixtures defined inline
- All warnings treated as errors (so no `DeprecationWarning`s allowed)
- 20-second timeout per test
- httpx normalizes response header names to lowercase

Reference test: `tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py` - creates a custom ASGI middleware class, registers with `app.add_middleware()`, uses `TestClient`.

## Requirements

1. **Algorithm**: Token bucket per client IP
2. **Config**: `requests_per_minute: int` (sustained rate), `burst_size: int` (max capacity)
3. **Rate limit exceeded**: HTTP 429 JSON response `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded", "retry_after": <int seconds>}`
4. **Headers on ALL responses** (including 429): `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
5. **Headers on 429 only**: `Retry-After`
6. **Export**: `RateLimitMiddleware` exported from `fastapi.middleware` package
7. **All existing tests must still pass**

## Key Technical Decisions to Address

1. Thread-safety: Use `asyncio.Lock` per bucket + lock for bucket-dict creation (double-checked locking)
2. IP extraction: Check `X-Forwarded-For` header first, fallback to `scope["client"][0]`, fallback to `"unknown"`
3. Header injection on allowed responses: Use `starlette.datastructures.MutableHeaders(scope=message)` in a `send` wrapper
4. `X-RateLimit-Reset`: Unix timestamp (int) of when the bucket will be full again (for allowed) or when next token is available (for 429)
5. Non-HTTP scopes (WebSocket, lifespan): pass through without rate limiting

## Files to Create/Modify

1. **CREATE** `fastapi/middleware/ratelimit.py`
2. **MODIFY** `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` (add export)
3. **CREATE** `tests/test_middleware_ratelimit.py`

Please design a detailed step-by-step implementation plan including:
- Complete implementation of `_TokenBucket` class (internal helper)
- Complete implementation of `RateLimitMiddleware` class
- The exact change to `__init__.py`
- A comprehensive test file covering: headers present, header values, remaining decrements, 429 on burst exceeded, 429 body format, 429 headers, per-IP independence (using X-Forwarded-For), reset timestamp is future, non-HTTP passthrough
- Any edge cases to handle (requests_per_minute=0, burst_size=0, unknown IP)

Focus on correctness, thread safety, and following existing code style.

57	def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
58	    return {"message": "OK"}
59	
60	
61	app.include_router(router)
62	app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
63	
64	
65	client = TestClient(app)
66	
67	
68	def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
69	    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
70	    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
71	    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))
72	
73	    with client:
74	        with open(path, "rb") as file:
75	            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
76	        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
77	        assert response.json() == {
78	            "detail": {
79	                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
80	                "code": 999,
81	                "message": "File limit exceeded",
82	            }
83	        }
84	
85	
86	def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
87	    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
88	    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")
89	
90	    with client:
91	        with open(path, "rb") as file:
92	            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
93	        assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
94	        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}
95

1	from collections.abc import Generator
2	from contextlib import contextmanager
3	from typing import Annotated, Any
4	
5	import pytest
6	from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, WebSocket
7	from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
8	
9	
10	class Session:
11	    def __init__(self) -> None:
12	        self.data = ["foo", "bar", "baz"]
13	        self.open = True
14	
15	    def __iter__(self) -> Generator[str, None, None]:
16	        for item in self.data:
17	            if self.open:
18	                yield item
19	            else:
20	                raise ValueError("Session closed")
21	
22	
23	@contextmanager
24	def acquire_session() -> Generator[Session, None, None]:
25	    session = Session()
26	    try:
27	        yield session
28	    finally:
29	        session.open = False
30	
31	
32	def dep_session() -> Any:
33	    with acquire_session() as s:
34	        yield s
35	
36	
37	def broken_dep_session() -> Any:
38	    with acquire_session() as s:
39	        s.open = False
40	        yield s
